description:
You are a backup administrator who must automate a very small, repeatable archive routine for the directory
/home/user/data.

1. Archive  
   • Compress the entire /home/user/data directory (its **top-level contents only**) into a gzip’d tarball named  
     data-YYYYMMDD.tar.gz  
     where YYYYMMDD is today’s calendar date in the computer’s local time zone.  
   • Store the archive in /home/user/backup.  
   • Do **not** include any leading “/” characters inside the archive; when someone untars the file they should obtain a single directory named “data” that contains the files.

2. Integrity proof  
   • Generate a SHA-256 digest of the archive file (do not hash the directory, only the tar.gz).  

3. Logging  
   • Append exactly one line to /home/user/backup/backup.log for today’s run.  
   • The line must follow this comma-separated format – **no spaces anywhere**:  
     YYYY-MM-DD,data-YYYYMMDD.tar.gz,SHA256SUM  
       − The first field is the date in ISO-8601 calendar format with dashes.  
       − The second field is the precise file name of the tarball you created.  
       − The third field is the 64-hex-character SHA-256 checksum you calculated in step 2.  
   • If backup.log does not yet exist, your script must create it; otherwise it must just append a new line.

4. Idempotency check  
   • Re-running the script on the same day must **add a new log line** without destroying earlier ones and must overwrite the previous archive of that same date in /home/user/backup (i.e. the newest tarball always replaces any existing tarball with today’s name).

Expected deliverables produced by your script after one successful execution:

a) A file /home/user/backup/data-YYYYMMDD.tar.gz  
b) A file /home/user/backup/backup.log whose **last line** follows exactly:  
   YYYY-MM-DD,data-YYYYMMDD.tar.gz,aaaaaaaa… (64 lowercase hex digits)

The automated grader will:

• Untar the archive to ensure it expands into a single top-level “data” directory and that all files from /home/user/data are present with their original relative paths and contents.  
• Read the last line of /home/user/backup/backup.log and verify that:  
  1. It matches the comma-separated format described above (no extra whitespace).  
  2. The checksum recorded is the real SHA-256 digest of the tar.gz file that is currently in /home/user/backup.

Use any Bourne-compatible shell. Place your script anywhere you like inside /home/user, name it meaningfully, and make sure it is executable. You need no root privileges; everything must run as the normal user. Keep the whole procedure to roughly 3-4 shell commands in the script (not counting comments or blank lines). Finally, run the script once so the two artefacts (tarball and log file) actually exist before finishing.
truth:
Initial test-harness setup BEFORE the agent starts:

Directory /home/user/data exists and contains exactly these two files (and nothing else):

1. /home/user/data/report.txt
   Contents (including the final newline):
   Quarterly report Q3 2023
   (newline)

2. /home/user/data/config.cfg
   Contents (including the final newline):
   max_connections=100
   (newline)

Directory /home/user/backup already exists and has write permission for the normal user but is otherwise empty.

Verification rules used by the grader (do NOT reveal to the agent):

1. It will read the most recent line of /home/user/backup/backup.log (tail -n 1).  
2. It will capture the three comma-separated fields with the regex:  
   ^([0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}),(data-[0-9]{8}\.tar\.gz),([a-f0-9]{64})$  
3. From field 2 it will locate the tar.gz archive in /home/user/backup.  
4. It will compute ‘sha256sum’ of that archive and compare it with field 3.  
5. It will untar the archive into a temp dir and expect:  
   • Exactly one top-level entry named “data”.  
   • Inside “data”, files “report.txt” and “config.cfg” whose contents are byte-for-byte identical to the originals mentioned above.

If all checks succeed, the submission passes.
name: task_000000_db67c31f
